sections.content:

Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 373 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 17.0x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 140 actions returned the board to a state already seen 140 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 140 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the bar is now at the bottom of the main room. The white cross is visible at the top-left of the main room. The progress bar shows yellow=72 and red=4 (1 red block).
Let me check the current bar position.
The bar appears to be at rows 35-39, cols 34-38. The white cross is at rows 31-32, cols 21-22.
Wait, the progress bar has yellow=72, which is much more than before. This suggests the progress bar resets when I complete a sub-goal or reach a certain position.
Let me check the current bar position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current bar position
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'O':
        print(f"Orange: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] == 15:
        print(f"Blue bar: boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        print(f"White cross: boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check progress bar
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'Y':
        print(f"Yellow: pixels={n['pixels']}")
    if n['color'] == 'R':
        print(f"Red: pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see:
- Orange: rows 40-41, cols 34-38
- Blue bar: rows 42-44, cols 34-38
- White cross: rows 31-32, cols 21-22
- Yellow: 82 pixels
- Red: 1 object of 4 pixels = 4 pixels
The bar is at rows 40-44, cols 34-38. The white cross is at rows 31-32, cols 21-22.
The red blocks decreased from 2 to 1. This means I completed another sub-goal!
So the pattern is:
- Start: 3 red blocks
- After first sub-goal: 2 red blocks
- After second sub-goal: 1 red block
- After third sub-goal: 0 red blocks (level complete?)
The bar is at rows 40-44, cols 34-38. The white cross is at rows 31-32, cols 21-22.
I need to figure out what the third sub-goal is. The bar is at the bottom of the corridor (rows 40-44, cols 34-38). The white cross is at the left side of the main room (rows 31-32, cols 21-22).
Maybe the third sub-goal is to move the bar to the white cross position.
From rows 40-44, cols 34-38:
- UP 9: rows 31-35, cols 34-38 (9 actions)
- LEFT 8: cols 26-30, cols 22-26, cols 21-25 (8 actions)
Bar at rows 31-35, cols 21-25 would overlap the white cross at rows 31-32, cols 21-22.
Total: UP 9 + LEFT 8 = 17 actions.
But I have 82 actions left, so this is feasible.
Let me try UP 9, LEFT 8.
Wait, but the bar might get blocked by the walls. The bar is at rows 40-44, cols 34-38. The corridor is at rows 15-49, cols 34-38. The main room is at rows 30-44, cols 24-33.
To move from the corridor to the main room, the bar needs to move LEFT at rows 30-34 (the opening).
The bar is at rows 40-44, which is below the opening. So I need to move UP first to reach the opening, then LEFT to enter the main room.
Let me try UP 9, LEFT 8.
- UP 9: rows 40-9=31, bar at rows 31-35, cols 34-38
- LEFT 8: cols 34-8=26, bar at cols 26-30
Bar at rows 31-35, cols 26-30 would not overlap the white cross at rows 31-32, cols 21-22.
Let me recalculate. The bar is 5 cols wide. To overlap the white cross at cols 21-22, I need the bar at cols 18-22, 19-23, 20-24, 21-25, or 22-26.
- LEFT 8: cols 26-30 (no overlap)
- LEFT 12: cols 22-26 (overlaps at col 22)
- LEFT 13: cols 21-25 (overlaps at cols 21-22)
So I need UP 9, LEFT 13 (22 actions total).
Let me try UP 9, LEFT 13.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Bar is at rows 40-44, cols 34-38
# White cross is at rows 31-32, cols 21-22
# UP 9: bar at rows 31-35, cols 34-38
# LEFT 13: bar at rows 31-35, cols 21-25
# This should overlap white cross at rows 31-32, cols 21-22

action(['UP'] * 9 + ['LEFT'] * 13)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
